title: Country Fried Steak
subtitle: Cube steak pan-fried in a seasoned crust, smothered in peppery cream gravy.

ingredients.heading: Ingredients
ingredients.note: Double-dredge breading and drippings-based gravy — both built in the same skillet.
items: 4 cube steaks (about 6 oz each, tenderized round steak)
1½ tsp kosher salt
1 tsp black pepper
1 tsp garlic powder
1 tsp onion powder
½ tsp smoked paprika
¼ tsp cayenne pepper (optional)
1½ cups all-purpose flour (1 cup for dredge + ½ cup seasoned for final coat)
2 large eggs
½ cup buttermilk
Vegetable oil for frying (~½ inch deep in skillet)
3 tbsp reserved pan drippings (for gravy)
3 tbsp all-purpose flour (for gravy roux)
2 cups whole milk
Salt + heavy black pepper to taste (for gravy)
ingredients.callout: Key rule: Press the breading firmly into the steak and let it rest 5 minutes before it hits the oil — this is what keeps the crust from falling off.

substitutes: Whole milk + 1 tsp white vinegar, rested 5 min

body.html: Cube steak double-dredged in seasoned flour, pan-fried until the crust is shatteringly crisp, then rested while you build a peppery cream gravy from the drippings in the same pan. One skillet, one classic.

items: Season + set up dredge — spice mix, three-bowl breading station
Bread the steaks — flour → egg wash → flour, press firmly, rest 5 min
Fry in batches — ½ inch oil, 350°F, don't crowd, flip once
Build cream gravy — roux from drippings, whisk in milk, pepper heavy
Plate + smother — ladle gravy over steak, serve with mashed potatoes or biscuits

body.html: The move: Rest the breaded steaks 5 min before frying. That pause is what glues the crust to the meat.

items: Pat cube steaks very dry with paper towels.
Combine salt, pepper, garlic powder, onion powder, paprika, and cayenne in a small bowl.
Season steaks on both sides with the spice mix — use it all.
Set up 3-bowl dredge station: 1 cup plain flour → 2 eggs + ½ cup buttermilk whisked together → ½ cup flour seasoned with a pinch of the same spice mix.

body.html: Season the flour too. A pinch of salt, pepper, and garlic powder in both flour stages adds flavor in every layer of the crust.

items: Dredge each steak in flour (bowl 1) — coat all sides, shake off excess.
Dip into egg-buttermilk wash (bowl 2) — let excess drip off.
Press into seasoned flour (bowl 3) — press firmly on both sides so it adheres.
Set breaded steaks on a wire rack or plate.
Let rest 5 minutes before frying — don't skip.

body.html: Press, don't dab. Firm pressure on that final flour coat is the difference between a crust that stays and one that slides into the oil.

items: Pour ~½ inch of oil into a large cast iron or heavy skillet.
Heat over medium-high until a pinch of flour dropped in sizzles immediately — about 350°F.
Fry steaks 2 at a time — don't crowd the pan.
Cook 3–4 min first side without moving until crust is deep golden brown.
Flip once → cook 2–3 min second side.
Transfer to a wire rack. Reserve 3 tbsp of the drippings for the gravy — pour off the rest.

body.html: Wire rack, not paper towels. Paper towels steam the crust soft. A rack keeps it crispy while you build the gravy.

items: With 3 tbsp drippings remaining in the skillet, lower heat to medium.
Whisk in 3 tbsp flour → stir constantly for 1–2 min to cook out the raw flour taste.
Slowly whisk in milk, ¼ cup at a time — work out any lumps before adding more.
Stir continuously until gravy thickens to a pourable, creamy consistency, 4–6 min.
Season with heavy black pepper and salt — taste and adjust.
If it gets too thick, add a splash of milk and stir.

body.html: Peppery is correct. Cream gravy should have a serious black pepper presence — not subtle. If it tastes mild, add more.

items: Place a fried steak on each plate.
Ladle cream gravy generously over the top — cover it.
Serve with mashed potatoes, biscuits, or white rice.
Put remaining gravy in a small pitcher or bowl on the table — there will be requests for more.

body.html: Don't be shy with the gravy. Country fried steak is smothered food. If the steak is still visible, you haven't added enough.
